era_context: The early 14th century continued to be marked by the fragmentation of Mongol power, the consolidation of European feudalism, and the early stages of climate deterioration. The Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada was stabilizing; the Mamluk Sultanate was at the height of its power; the Papacy remained in Avignon; and the Scottish Wars of Independence were in their final phases. This was a period of relative stability before the Great Famine of 1314–1317.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asian kingdoms (sparse documentation in Western sources)
Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources beyond Mongol chronicles)

events.title: Council of Vienne concludes; Knights Templar formally dissolved
events.description: The Council of Vienne, which had begun in 1311, concluded with the formal dissolution of the Knights Templar. Pope Clement V, under pressure from King Philip IV of France, issued papal bulls suppressing the order and transferring its properties to the Knights Hospitaller (though Philip seized much of the French holdings). The Templar suppression marked the triumph of secular royal authority over a major religious-military institution and demonstrated the vulnerability of the Church to political pressure.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay, Grand Master of the Templars

sources.name: Papal bulls and council records (primary documents)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Vienne's conclusion and the formal dissolution of the Templars are well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader conflict between papacy and secular rulers

events.title: Henry VII, Holy Roman Emperor, dies
events.description: Henry VII (Henry of Luxembourg), Holy Roman Emperor, died on 24 August 1313 in Buonconvento, Italy. His reign (1312–1313) had been marked by an attempt to restore imperial authority in Italy and to assert the independence of the Empire from papal control. His death triggered a succession dispute between rival German princes and left the Empire without a clear leader for several years. The succession crisis weakened imperial authority and contributed to the fragmentation of German politics.
key_figures: Robert of Anjou, King of Naples
events.certainty_note: Henry VII's death date (24 August 1313) is well-documented. The succession dispute that followed is attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Led to 1314-001 (Succession dispute and election of Louis IV)

events.title: Battle of Marj al-Rahit: Mamluk victory over Mongol forces
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad defeated a Mongol invasion force at Marj al-Rahit, consolidating Mamluk control of the Levant and preventing Mongol expansion into Egypt. The victory demonstrated the military superiority of the Mamluks and secured their position as the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean. This battle was part of a broader pattern of Mamluk-Mongol conflict that had defined the late 13th and early 14th centuries.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (Mamluk chronicle, compiled 15th century)
Ibn al-Athir and other Arabic chronicles

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Mamluk chronicles, but the exact date and details are somewhat uncertain. Some sources place it in 1313; others suggest 1312 or 1314. The outcome (Mamluk victory) is clear, but tactical details are sparse.
cross_references: Consolidates Mamluk power in the Levant

events.title: Mansa Musa begins reign of Mali Empire
events.description: Mansa Musa ascended to the throne of the Mali Empire, beginning a reign that would last until 1337 and transform Mali into one of the wealthiest and most powerful states in Africa. Musa's reign was marked by the expansion of Mali's territory, the promotion of Islam and Islamic scholarship, the construction of mosques and madrasas, and the famous pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324. His reign represented the height of Mali's power and influence in West Africa.
events.certainty_note: The exact date of Mansa Musa's accession is uncertain; sources vary between 1312 and 1314. The date 1313 is conventional but approximate. His reign's end (1337) is more secure. The sources are non-contemporary and often written decades or centuries later.
cross_references: Led to 1324-001 (Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca)
Part of broader Mali Empire expansion (1300–1350)

events.title: Execution of Jacques de Molay, Grand Master of the Knights Templar
events.description: Jacques de Molay, the last Grand Master of the Knights Templar, was executed by burning on 18 March 1313 (some sources say 1314) on an island in the Seine River in Paris. His execution followed years of torture and imprisonment at the behest of King Philip IV. De Molay's death marked the final destruction of the Templar order and symbolized the triumph of secular royal authority over the Church. According to legend, de Molay cursed both Philip IV and Pope Clement V to meet him before God's judgment—both died within a year.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay, Grand Master of the Knights Templar
events.certainty_note: De Molay's execution is well-documented, though the exact date is disputed between 1313 and 1314. Most modern scholarship favors 18 March 1313, but some sources place it in 1314. The event itself is uncontested.
cross_references: Follows from 1311-001 (Council of Vienne and Templar suppression)
Part of broader conflict between papacy and secular rulers

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Jacques de Molay's execution is disputed: while 18 March 1313 is widely cited, some sources place it in 1314. The legend of de Molay's curse is almost certainly apocryphal; both Philip IV and Clement V did die within a year, but this may be coincidence rather than fulfillment of a curse. The Battle of Marj al-Rahit's date is uncertain; some sources place it in 1312 or 1314 rather than 1313. Mansa Musa's accession date is approximate; the exact year is not securely established in contemporary sources. Henry VII's death is well-documented, but his significance as a Holy Roman Emperor is debated; his reign was brief (1312–1313) and his Italian campaign was ultimately unsuccessful.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1313 is strong for Western Europe (France, the Papacy, the Holy Roman Empire) due to abundant monastic chronicles and royal records. Arabic sources on the Mamluk Sultanate are detailed but compiled retrospectively. Mali Empire sources are sparse and non-contemporary; most information comes from later Arab geographers and European travelers. The execution of Jacques de Molay is well-documented in Western sources but reflects a Western perspective on the Templar suppression. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Indian subcontinent are underrepresented. Bias toward political and military events is inherent in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Council of Vienne (1311–1313) formally dissolved the Knights Templar.
De Molay's execution was the final act in the suppression of the Templars.
Henry VII's death triggered a succession dispute in the Holy Roman Empire.
Mansa Musa's reign led to his famous pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324.
